title: New defect warranty law: what every owner needs to know before selling
description: Since 1 January 2026, a major revision of the Code of Obligations has come into force. It concerns the warranty against defects in real estate sales and construction contracts, and profoundly changes the rules for property sellers.
content:
**February 2026**

## New defect warranty law: what every owner needs to know before selling

Since 1 January 2026, a major revision of the Code of Obligations has come into force. It concerns the warranty against defects in real estate sales and construction contracts, and profoundly changes the rules for property sellers.

At GARY, we believe every owner concerned must understand what has changed and, above all, how to prepare for it.

## Background: why this reform?

Until the end of 2025, when a developer or owner sold a property, it was common to include a clause in the sale contract excluding liability for defects. In return, the buyer would receive the seller's warranty rights against the companies that carried out the work.

In theory, the buyer could pursue the subcontractors. In practice, it was often an uphill battle: identifying the right contact, proving liability, dealing with potential company bankruptcies… The system was unbalanced.

Adopted in December 2024 by the Federal Assembly, this revision of the Code of Obligations aims to restore the balance between sellers and buyers. It came into force on 1 January 2026, for all contracts signed from that date onwards.

## What changes in practice

### A right to repair that is now mandatory

This is the major change. Before this reform, the buyer of a property could only demand two things in case of a defect: a price reduction or, in serious cases, contract termination. They could not ask the seller to carry out repairs.

From now on, the buyer has a right to rectification. They can require the seller to cover the cost of repairing defects. And this right is mandatory: any contractual clause attempting to exclude or restrict it is simply null and void.

### Notification period extended to 60 days

Previously, case law set the defect notification period at just 7 days. Barely enough for a non-professional who gradually discovers a problem with their property. The new law extends this period to 60 days from the discovery of the defect. This period cannot be shortened by contract.

### A locked-in limitation period

The limitation period remains set at 5 years for building works. However, it can no longer be contractually reduced to the detriment of the buyer or project owner.

### Who is affected?

This reform applies to new constructions or those completed less than two years before the sale. It covers all contracts concluded from 1 January 2026, including purchase agreements signed in 2025 where the transfer of ownership occurs after that date.

## Points of vigilance for property sellers

This law is a major step forward for buyers. But for sellers of recent properties, it requires immediate adaptation.

**Have your contracts reviewed.** Old templates containing warranty exclusion clauses are now obsolete. Your notary must incorporate the new mandatory provisions into any new sale contract.

**Beware of warranty rights assignments.** If you assign your warranty rights against subcontractors to the buyer, but the buyer still turns against you — which is now their right — you will no longer have recourse against the responsible companies. It's a double penalty.

**Document the condition of your property.** With a notification period extended to 60 days and a mandatory right to repair, proof of the property's condition at the time of sale becomes your best protection.

**Plan ahead for purchase agreements.** If you sign a purchase agreement in 2025 but the transfer of ownership occurs in 2026, the new law applies. Your notary must take this into account from the drafting of the agreement.

## GARY's view

This reform is a step in the right direction. It protects buyers, holds the entire real estate chain accountable and puts an end to contractual practices that had become unbalanced.

For sellers, it's a paradigm shift that requires rigorous support. At GARY, we integrate these new provisions into our advice at every stage of the sale, from valuation to signing at the notary's office.

Are you considering selling a recent property and want to understand the impact of this law on your situation? Contact us to discuss it.

title: Geneva real estate market: why preparation makes the difference
description: In a real estate market as competitive as Geneva's, a common misconception persists: the property always goes to the highest bidder. In reality, this is no longer systematically the case. Today, the property very often goes to the best-prepared buyer.
content:
**February 2026**

## Geneva real estate market: why preparation makes the difference

In a real estate market as competitive as Geneva's, a common misconception persists: the property always goes to the highest bidder.

In reality, this is no longer systematically the case.

Today, the property very often goes to the best-prepared buyer.

At GARY, we observe that the strength of an application can make the difference, even against a financially superior offer.

## Context: a market where security comes first

The tension in the Geneva market is well known. The scarcity of properties and strong demand create situations of direct competition between buyers.

In this context, sellers are no longer solely seeking to maximise the price. They attach growing importance to the security and reliability of the transaction.

Between two offers — one higher but uncertain, the other perfectly structured — the choice often leans towards the most secure option.

## The decisive factors today

**Financing** is the first point of attention. A buyer able to present pre-approved financing immediately strengthens the credibility of their offer. Using a responsive mortgage broker, such as Plus Groupe, often allows for faster approval and a broader view than through traditional banking institutions.

**The responsiveness of partners**, particularly brokers and notaries, also plays a central role. In a competitive environment, speed of execution is decisive. A file ready to be signed constitutes a real advantage.

**Mastery of timelines**, particularly those related to the 2nd pillar (pension fund), is another key factor. Insufficient anticipation can slow down or even jeopardise a transaction, while a perfectly managed file reassures the seller.

**Flexibility on the signing date** can prove decisive. Many sellers are themselves engaged in an acquisition process. Their decision therefore also depends on their own schedule.

## Points of vigilance for buyers

In this context, preparation is no longer an advantage — it's a prerequisite.

- Anticipate and validate your financing in advance
- Surround yourself with responsive partners
- Master all timelines
- Adopt strategic flexibility

All elements that help secure an offer and maximise your chances.

## GARY's view

The Geneva real estate market has evolved. Price remains an important factor, but it is no longer sufficient on its own.

The quality of the application, execution capability and perceived reliability have become decisive levers in decision-making.

At GARY, we support our clients in this approach by structuring solid, credible and immediately actionable files.

In Geneva, preparation is no longer a competitive advantage.

It determines the outcome of the negotiation.

title: Mortgage rates in Switzerland: where do we stand and how to take advantage of them in 2026?
description: Analysis of current mortgage rates in Switzerland within the 2026 economic context. The SNB maintains its key rate at 0%, inflation remains stable below 1%, and financing conditions remain historically favourable for buyers and refinancing operations.
content:
## Current context: Historically low rates

The Swiss National Bank's key interest rate stands at 0%. After a series of cuts initiated in 2024 and completed by mid-2025, the SNB has stabilised its monetary policy. This reflects a major achievement: "inflation has returned to the price stability zone" between 0% and 2%, currently below 1%.

The key rate represents the borrowing cost for banks from the SNB. When it is low, it reduces financing costs, directly affecting fixed-rate and SARON-indexed mortgages.

## Forecasts for 2026: Likely stability

Markets anticipate "a continuation of low-rate conditions in 2026" despite modest recent increases in Swiss bond yields. Supporting factors include:

- Inflation forecast at around 0.3% in 2026
- Moderate economic growth (~0.9%)
- Reduced slowdown risks thanks to the Switzerland-United States trade agreement

The SNB is expected to maintain its rate at 0%. Rate increases seem unlikely, as do significant cuts. SARON mortgages should remain stable, while medium- and long-term fixed rates may experience slight fluctuations without major changes.

## Impact of rates on your mortgage

The mortgage rate determines:
- Monthly payments
- Total cost of credit
- Financial flexibility

Post-2022 inflation led to sharp rate increases; today's reverse situation — lower inflation enabling rate relief — creates favourable conditions for buyers and refinancing.

## Key factors influencing rates

Swiss mortgage rates depend on multiple factors:
- The SNB key interest rate
- Bank refinancing costs
- Inflation levels
- Swiss and international economic conditions
- US Federal Reserve policy

A comprehensive, contextualised analysis is essential — rates alone reveal little.

## Choosing the right mortgage model

Adapt your mortgage choice to your personal risk tolerance:

- Do you prefer budget certainty? "A fixed-rate mortgage offers security and stability"
- Can you accept fluctuations with financial reserves? SARON mortgages may offer long-term advantages
- Many situations benefit from mixed strategies

The substantial investment amounts in Geneva justify personalised guidance.

## GARY's recommendations for 2026

1. Think beyond current rates
2. Assess your personal risk tolerance, not just your financial capacity
3. Maximise your down payment to secure better rates
4. Obtain professional advice — skilled negotiation saves tens of thousands

## FAQ

**Will rates rise sharply in 2026?**
Unlikely. The base scenario remains one of durably low rates.

**SARON or fixed rate today?**
It depends on your personal situation, not just forecasts.

**Is now the right time to buy in Geneva?**
With favourable financing conditions and structural demand, yes — with the right guidance.

title: CECB: the smart move and hidden advantage before selling or renovating your property
description: The CECB is the official energy label for buildings in Switzerland. This certificate evaluates envelope efficiency, energy performance and CO2 emissions, with classes ranging from A to G. An essential document before any sale or renovation.
content:
## What is the CECB?

The CECB is Switzerland's official energy label for buildings. It applies to all types of residential properties, collective buildings, administrative structures, commercial spaces, hotels and other types of facilities.

The certification evaluates three main dimensions:

**Building envelope efficiency** — This examines wall insulation, roof covering, floor treatments, window quality, thermal bridges and other factors determining heat retention or loss.

**Overall energy performance** — This assesses energy requirements for heating, domestic hot water, electricity consumption and building systems. Renewable energy sources or heat pump installations can improve the rating.

**Direct CO2 emissions** — Added during the 2023 update, this indicator has become increasingly significant in climate discussions.

## Classification system

Buildings receive ratings ranging from **Class A** (highly efficient) to **Class G** (poor performance/high consumption).

## Why should property owners consider it?

Although some cantons such as Geneva do not require CECB certification for all transactions, discerning property owners recognise its strategic value. The certification provides documented evidence of a property's energy characteristics — a factor influencing buyer confidence, financing terms and potential renovation priorities.

## Advantages for selling

A good CECB rating can:
- Reassure buyers about the property's energy quality
- Justify a higher sale price
- Accelerate the transaction by reducing uncertainties
- Differentiate your property from the competition

## Advantages for renovation

The CECB identifies:
- Weak points in the building envelope
- Energy improvement priorities
- Energy savings potential
- Eligibility for cantonal and federal subsidies

## How to obtain a CECB?

The certification is carried out by accredited experts who:
1. Inspect the building
2. Analyse plans and technical documentation
3. Calculate energy performance
4. Issue the official certificate

The process typically takes 2-4 weeks and costs between CHF 800 and CHF 2,000 depending on the size and complexity of the property.

title: Real Insiders x GARY: the vision behind the agency
description: Interview with Gregory Autieri, CEO and Partner at GARY. Discussion about his journey from commodity trading to real estate brokerage, the agency's vision, and how former traders have become major real estate investors in Geneva.
content:
## About the episode

This episode of the Real Insiders podcast features Gregory Autieri, Partner & CEO of the GARY real estate agency, on the Real Insiders platform.

## Key topics discussed

The episode explores Autieri's transition from commodity trading to real estate brokerage and discusses the founding of GARY in 2023. The agency has attracted attention through its distinctive visual identity and unconventional approach to real estate services.

## Episode highlights

The podcast discussion covers:

- Autieri's professional journey from trading to real estate entrepreneurship
- GARY's founding vision and its underlying ambitions
- The strategic importance of communication and branding in modern real estate agencies
- The emerging trend of former traders becoming major real estate investors in Geneva
- Building success without an extensive network
- GARY's new offering for real estate development projects
- The agency's 10-year vision
- Brands that inspire the direction
- Practical advice for aspiring entrepreneurs

## Notable quote

Autieri states: *"I can confirm 100%, traders in Geneva are major players in real estate acquisitions"* — highlighting the importance of this professional demographic in the local property market.

## Where to listen

The episode is available on several platforms:
- Official Real Insiders website
- Spotify
- Apple Podcasts

## Why this episode matters

This interview offers a rare insight into the strategy of a modern real estate agency that defies conventions. Gregory Autieri shares candidly about his career transition, the challenges encountered and his vision for revolutionising real estate brokerage in Geneva.

For entrepreneurs, investors and real estate professionals, this episode offers valuable perspectives on how to build a strong, differentiating brand in a traditional industry.

title: Interview by AGEFI IMMO
description: Interview with Gregory Autieri, co-founder of GARY, by AGEFI Immo. A look back at two years of success: rapid growth, a new real estate development model and bold communication that has made GARY a recognised player in French-speaking Switzerland's real estate market.
content:
## Two years of success

*The GARY agency celebrated its second anniversary in late October with an evening at its offices in Geneva. Co-founder Gregory Autieri looks back at what characterises this young real estate player: rapid growth, a new development model and bold communication.*

## What is your assessment of these first two years of activity?

A very positive assessment, as the company is in full growth. Our revenue and staff numbers doubled between 2024 and 2025: we now have around ten people. We have successfully established the brand. GARY is now recognised within the French-speaking Swiss real estate ecosystem, which was not easy since the name does not directly refer to real estate. We focused on digital communication, producing a great deal of video content, photos and information. On Instagram, we are among the leaders in French-speaking Switzerland, perhaps even in Switzerland as a whole, in terms of interactions and views. Everything is going very well, both in real estate and in marketing.

## You have a particular style in your communication, the GARY tone...

We are not trying to reinvent everything, but rather to take what exists and adapt it to our vision by breaking the mould. This approach may surprise, but our priority is always quality of service. To have the best reputation, you need the best service, the best product and the best execution.

## How are your activities structured?

Our core business remains transactions, with a team of five brokers between Geneva and Terre-Sainte. We are currently consolidating our base in Geneva, recruiting for other departments to strengthen our foundations. Today, approximately 80% of our activity concerns resale and 20% development. Ultimately, the goal would be to reach a 50/50 balance.

## What is your analysis of the sales market?

The market remains dynamic, but it is becoming highly professionalised. Buyers are well informed, sellers are attentive and competition between brokers is fierce. It is not the speed of sale that makes the difference, but the ability to evaluate well, to advise and to support over the long term.

## Don't high prices limit access to property ownership?

It is certain that slightly lower prices would facilitate access, especially for young people. After the significant increase during and after Covid, we are currently in a stabilisation phase. However, in Geneva, space is limited, so scarcity maintains property values. But we are not in a speculative system like in other countries, which prevents violent crises.

## Regarding development, you have launched a new product. What is the principle?

Until now, the standard approach has always been for the developer to make an offer to the landowner, hoping it would be accepted. We propose a development model where the interests of the owner and the developer are aligned. The agency takes charge of studies, development and permits, then defines the project according to the market to optimise its value. There is no traditional purchase commitment: fees are paid according to progress, and the owner recovers the majority of the final product, in a win-win approach. We first secured plots to prove the model worked before presenting it publicly. We have been doing this for a short time and we receive numerous spontaneous calls from property owners.

## Do you plan to expand the agency to other cantons or mountain areas?

Yes, but the timing depends first on consolidating our base in Geneva. We are investing in our internal systems, technology, AI, and we are recruiting to build a solid foundation. Expansion will then depend on human opportunities, as you need to find the right people in each city. The goal is to open in 2026.

title: Selling alone or with a professional? The choice that determines the success of your property sale.
description: When deciding to sell a property, one question comes up immediately: should you entrust the sale to a professional or handle it yourself?
content:
**November 2025**

When deciding to sell a property, one question comes up immediately: **should you entrust the sale to a professional or handle it yourself?**

Behind this choice lie two very different approaches... and significant consequences for the price, timeline and security of your transaction.

At GARY, we support property owners in selling their homes and we primarily observe **that being well supported changes everything**. So, what are the concrete differences between a professional sale and a private sale?

## Real estate market expertise

A broker has thorough knowledge of the **local market**: price per sqm, trends, sought-after neighbourhoods, demand evolution... Their role: to value your property at the right price, to sell **quickly** and under the **best conditions**.

Conversely, selling on your own means setting your price without expertise.

A mistake is costly:

Too high: the property stays on the market and becomes "stale".

Too low: immediate financial loss.

## A structured marketing strategy

With a professional, you benefit from a **comprehensive** strategy: professional photography, property staging, marketing tools, targeted distribution...

The objective: to generate maximum interest to maximise the sale price.

In a private sale, you must manage everything yourself. Without marketing knowledge, it is easy to lack visibility or use ineffective channels, which can slow the sale or reduce its perceived value.

## Visibility and buyer network

A broker showcases your property to a wide network:

- Specialised platforms
- Qualified buyer database
- Network of partners and professional contacts

Result: more visibility = more viewings = more offers.

In a private sale, reach is generally more **limited**, which often extends the sales timeline.

## Buyer screening and viewing management

Entrusting your sale also means saving **time** and gaining **peace of mind**.

The broker filters enquiries, selects creditworthy profiles, organises and conducts viewings, then provides a report.

In a private sale, you must manage everything: calls, messages, viewings, profile screening... A time-consuming process, sometimes stressful, especially if you have a professional career alongside.

## Negotiation: a key skill

A broker is trained to **defend** your interests and **optimise** the final price, while maintaining a serene relationship with buyers.

Privately, negotiation can become emotional or clumsy. A frequent result: accepting an offer that is too low, or conversely losing a buyer by mishandling the discussion.

## Conclusion

Entrusting the sale of your property to a professional means choosing **peace of mind**. You benefit from **comprehensive** support that maximises your sale price, secures each step of the process and saves you precious time. Conversely, selling on your own may seem economical at first glance, but the lack of expertise, network and negotiation skills can quickly lead to **costly mistakes**.

title: Imputed rental value: what are the consequences after the vote?
description: By Jonathan, partner at Plus Financement, GARY partner, exclusive interview
content:
**October 2025**

*By Jonathan, partner at Plus Financement, GARY partner, exclusive interview*

## Context: a game-changing reform

Switzerland has just reached a historic milestone: citizens have voted for **the abolition of imputed rental value** and, crucially, **of all tax deductions related to property renovations**.

This reform only applies to owner-occupied dwellings, whether primary or secondary residences. Rental properties are therefore not affected.

In practical terms, this means that **property owners will no longer be able to deduct renovation work** on their property from their taxes.

This decision, which will take full effect by 2028, is transforming **investment strategies, renovation plans and the resale market**.

In the short term, a clear dynamic is emerging: everyone wants to renovate or sell before the deadline. But in the medium term, the effects could be more mixed.

We interviewed **Jonathan, partner at Plus Financement**, to analyse the impact of this vote on the Swiss property market, and particularly in Geneva.

## Until 2028: the rush to renovate

The decision to abolish tax deductions for renovation work creates a short-term windfall effect.

In practical terms, **all property owners now have an incentive to renovate before 2028**, while they can still benefit from these deductions.

This means an **explosion in demand** for everything related to renovation, construction and materials.

Construction companies will operate at full capacity, at least until the new law comes into force.

But this movement also affects the transaction market. Many owners may choose to **sell now**, so that their buyers can benefit from the tax deductions on renovation work. An older property will therefore be more attractive today than it will be tomorrow.

## After 2028: the existing property market under pressure

Once the deductions are abolished, the equation changes radically. Without tax incentives, **owners will have less reason to renovate**, and **buyers may shun older properties** in favour of new builds, which are more modern, more energy efficient and free from regulatory surprises.

Probable outcome:

- A decline in prices in the existing property segment
- A slowdown in the construction sector
- And, potentially, an increase in undeclared work, as some seek to reduce costs without benefiting from official deductions

The fiscal and economic consequences could be significant: less VAT collected, less declared activity, and a generally duller market.

## The role of cantons: the last card to play

Even though the Confederation has abolished general deductions, **each canton will be able to decide whether to maintain targeted incentives**, particularly for energy renovations or installations using renewable energy.

This point will be decisive: without these deductions, owners will be less inclined to invest in sustainable renovations, which would run counter to Switzerland's carbon neutrality goals.

We are therefore navigating blind: the coming months will be crucial in determining whether Geneva and other cantons will implement intelligent transitional measures.

## Buy or wait? The key question

Should you buy now or wait until after 2028?

For Jonathan, the answer is clear:

"In real estate, when you can buy, you should buy. Prices have always tended to rise."

In other words: **the right time is when you are ready**.

But he qualifies:

- If your dream is to acquire a detached house, do it before 2028. These properties are becoming rare, and renovating them while deductions still exist remains a strategic choice.
- If you prefer a new, energy-efficient home, the post-2028 market will suit you better: high energy performance apartments, with low energy consumption, aligned with new environmental standards.

## And for sellers?

For property owners considering selling within the next 3 to 5 years, **timing is crucial**. Today's buyers are still motivated by the possibility of deducting renovation costs. After 2028, **this argument will disappear**, making the sale of an older property more complex but not impossible.

In summary: **sell while deductions still exist, buy while the market is dynamic**. The 2024-2028 window could well be the most strategic of the decade.

## Conclusion

The Swiss property market is entering a unique period of transition. In the short term, intense activity. In the medium term, an inevitable rebalancing.

Property owners, buyers, investors: **the next four years will be decisive**. More than ever, strategy rather than reaction is needed. And at **GARY Real Estate**, we are here to guide you through these complex choices, with clarity, rigour and vision.

title: Valuing your property: how is the calculation done?
description: When deciding to sell or buy a property, one question always comes first: what is its true value? Behind this figure, which may seem simple, lies a rigorous and precise valuation process.
content:
**September 2025**

When deciding to sell or buy a property, one question always comes first: **what is its true value?** Behind this figure, which may seem simple, lies a rigorous and precise valuation process.

At GARY, we know that this step is much more than a calculation: **it is the key to a successful transaction**. So, how does a broker determine the value of a house or an apartment?

## Two pillars: the land and the construction

A property's value rests primarily on two components: **the land** and **the construction**.

The land: its location is **decisive**. For example, a 1,000 sqm plot in Cologny obviously does not have the same value as an identical plot situated beneath the airport flight path. The location, views, sun exposure, neighbourhood tranquillity and proximity to amenities directly influence the price per square metre (sqm).

The house or building: here, the logic is different. Using the same example, a 200 sqm house costs the same to build, whether in Cologny or near the airport. Its value is assessed per cubic metre (cbm), taking into account materials, architectural style and level of finish.

## Depreciation: when time leaves its mark

A brand-new villa and a 20-year-old house, even if they appear similar at first glance, do not have the same value. Why? Because over time, a property undergoes what is known as **depreciation**.

In other words, each year of use slightly reduces the construction's value, unless significant renovations have been carried out. This analysis reflects reality: a house lived in for two decades cannot be valued at the same price as an identical house freshly completed.

## Outbuildings and exterior features

The valuation does not stop at the walls and the land. Every ancillary element counts and can significantly affect the final value.

Among them:

- Swimming pools
- Pool houses
- Garages or parking spaces
- Terraces or pergolas
- Landscaped gardens, with plantings, mature trees or specific features

These "extras" add real (and perceived) value to the property, as they directly contribute to the comfort and quality of life for future occupants.

## The broker's method: a detailed report

A serious valuation is not simply a quick glance or a rapid comparison with the neighbourhood. The broker prepares a detailed report that takes into account:

- The price of land per sqm according to the zone
- The value of the construction per cbm, adjusted for depreciation
- Specific outbuildings and features
- References from recent sales in the area (for example, similar apartments sold in recent months)
- Properties currently on the market, which reflect competition and local trends

It is this combination of hard data and on-the-ground expertise that makes all the difference.

## Why a professional valuation is essential

Many property owners are tempted to set the price of their home themselves, often based on what they "would like" to receive. However, the property market does not operate on wishes but on a value equation.

**A price that is too high**: the property remains on the market, viewings become scarce, and potential buyers end up having doubts.

**A price that is too low**: you lose part of the true value of your assets.

With a professional valuation, you benefit from an objective perspective, supported by technical criteria and thorough knowledge of the Geneva market. In Geneva, for example, buyer expectations differ between a city-centre apartment and a villa in a residential municipality. An experienced broker will guide you to optimise the presentation of your property without exceeding your budget.

## Conclusion

Valuing a property is a **balancing act** between science and experience. Between the value of the land, the quality of construction, depreciation and the addition of exterior features, **every detail counts**.

At GARY, our role is to transform this data into a fair and well-supported valuation, so as to offer you the **best chances of selling quickly and at the right price**. Because beyond the numbers, a well-executed valuation is above all the guarantee of securing your property project.

title: Renovate or sell as-is: what is the best strategy?
description: It is a question that almost all property owners ask themselves when putting their house or apartment on the market: should I renovate before selling? The answer is not always a simple "yes" or "no".
content:
**August 2025**

It is a question that almost all property owners ask themselves when putting their house or apartment on the market: **should I renovate before selling?** The answer is not always a simple "yes" or "no".

In reality, it is above all about **finding the right balance** between showcasing your property and **avoiding unnecessary expenses**. Here is what you need to know.

## The basic principle: attract without over-investing

The objective before a sale is simple: **present your property in its best light**. Like a used car, a good clean, a strong presentation and an impression of care make all the difference.

However, embarking on major works (changing the kitchen, completely redoing the bathroom...) is rarely a good idea. Why? Because these rooms are very personal and each buyer will have their own tastes. What seems modern and practical to you may not suit the future buyer at all.

## The "visible" renovations that make a difference

There are, however, a few strategic adjustments that are well worth making:

- A general refresh: repainting walls in light, neutral tones, repairing visible cracks, revarnishing woodwork.
- Attention to floors: replacing cracked tiles, sanding a parquet floor or laying a simple, clean covering.
- Maintaining exteriors: a trimmed garden, a clean terrace, a welcoming entrance immediately give a good impression.

These small renovations have a dual advantage: they **improve the overall appearance** and above all, they **reassure the buyer**. A filled crack, a removed stain or a replaced handle shows that the property has been well maintained.

Conversely, defects left as they are can raise fears of more serious problems, even if they are only minor details. These interventions **do not require a large budget**, but they **strengthen the perceived value of the property**.

## The golden rule: timelessness

When it comes to property sales, **neutrality** is your best ally. An interior that is too heavily marked by your personal tastes risks limiting the love-at-first-sight reaction from buyers.

Favour sober tones, simple materials, and avoid overly "trendy" choices that could quickly go out of fashion. The idea is to offer a clear, neutral base in which visitors can **easily picture themselves**.

This is what is called **timelessness**: a style that remains pleasant and attractive, regardless of changing fashions.

## What if you don't renovate at all?

Let us be clear: it is **not mandatory to renovate in order to sell**. A property can find a buyer even with an ageing kitchen or bathroom, provided the price reflects this. Some buyers actually prefer to purchase "as-is" to carry out the work themselves and adapt the space to their preferences.

What matters most is not leaving visible defects untreated: a visible crack, a stained carpet, a broken handle can give the impression of a neglected property, which reduces interest and therefore offers.

## The broker's role: advising before listing

Every property is unique, and there is no universal formula. This is why the broker's role is essential:

- Analysing the condition of the property
- Identifying renovations that can genuinely boost attractiveness
- Avoiding unnecessary expenditure
- And above all, placing everything in the context of the local market

In Geneva, for example, buyer expectations differ between a city-centre apartment and a villa in a residential municipality. An experienced broker will guide you to optimise the presentation of your property without exceeding your budget.

## Conclusion

Renovating before selling is not an obligation, but a value-enhancement strategy. An intelligent, neutral and timeless refresh can be enough to attract buyers, while major renovations are often best avoided.

At GARY, our mission is to support you in these choices: showcasing what truly matters, simplifying the preparation of your property and enabling you to sell under the best conditions. Because a successful sale always begins with a fair, polished and strategic presentation.

title: Selling your property during a divorce: what you need to know
description: When a shared story comes to an end, the stakes often go beyond the emotional. The shared property quickly becomes a central issue — at once legal, financial and symbolic.
content:
**May 2025**

When a shared story comes to an end, **the stakes often go beyond the emotional**. The shared property quickly becomes a central issue — at once legal, financial and symbolic.

Should you sell? Who decides? How do you protect your interests without aggravating tensions? At GARY, we support numerous couples through this delicate stage every year (11 couples in 2024), with a clear objective: **to transform a sensitive situation into a structured and balanced transition**.

## Why sell during a divorce?

In most cases, the property represents the couple's main asset. During a divorce, three main options are available to the owners:

- One party buys out the other's share.
- The property is retained by both ex-spouses, who remain co-owners for an indefinite period.
- The property is sold and the proceeds are shared.

Selling remains the most common option, particularly when a buyout is impossible for financial reasons or when relations between the parties are difficult. Selling allows you to turn the page, liquidate the joint asset and start afresh on clear terms.

## Timing: a strategic decision

Selling in a highly emotional context is always a challenge. But timing is crucial. You need to consider both **the state of the Geneva property market**, **seasonality** (some months are more favourable than others), and the **legal situation** of your divorce (separation of assets or matrimonial regime?).

Our role at GARY is precisely to advise you on the best time to list, in order to **maximise your property's value — even in an urgent situation**.

## Beware of emotional co-ownership

A separation often severely tests the ability to cooperate. Yet selling a property requires joint decisions: choosing the price, signing the mandate, approving offers... Without mediation or neutral support, the sale can quickly become a battleground.

Our experience as a trusted third party enables us to assume this role of professional intermediary. We know how to communicate with both parties, defuse tensions and make progress despite disagreements.

## What documents should you prepare?

Before listing your property on the market, make sure you have:

- The divorce judgment or agreement (if one already exists)
- The marriage contract (to determine each party's rights)
- The property valuation (contact us)
- Mortgage statements
- Agreement from both parties for the sale

At GARY, we support you in compiling this file, so as to gain efficiency from the very first steps.

## What are the tax implications of a divorce-related sale?

The sale of a property may result in capital gains tax. **Good news**: in the case of divorce, certain exemptions or tax deferrals are possible, particularly when one spouse buys out the other's share as part of the divorce or judicial separation. Furthermore, property transfers between spouses carried out as part of the liquidation of the matrimonial regime may benefit from an **exemption from transfer duties**.

As every situation is unique, we work with partner tax specialists to provide you with tailor-made, clear expertise with no unpleasant surprises.

## Our added value in these sensitive moments

GARY is not just a real estate agency. It is a human, responsive team trained to manage sales in contexts of tension or high emotional charge. We know that this is not just about a property to sell, but about a life project to rebuild.

We offer you a tailor-made, neutral, confidential and respectful approach, with one objective: **to complete the sale under the best conditions, so you can move forward and turn the page with complete peace of mind**.

## Exclusive partnership with divorce.ch

NEW: GARY is an official partner of divorce.ch

This partnership enables us to offer you even more comprehensive support, in conjunction with specialists in divorce, family law and mediation. Discover our dedicated page: gary.ch/divorce

title: IBGI: What every property owner needs to know about capital gains tax on real estate
description: Selling a property in Geneva can be a lucrative operation... but beware, a portion of the gain goes to the state. This tax is called the IBGI — the tax on real estate profit and capital gains. If you are considering selling your apartment, house or land, it is essential to fully understand what this entails.
content:
**May 2025**

Selling a property in Geneva can be a lucrative operation... but beware, a portion of the gain goes to the state. This tax is called the IBGI — **the tax on real estate profit and capital gains**. If you are considering selling your apartment, house or land, it is essential to fully understand what this entails to avoid unpleasant surprises.

## What exactly is the IBGI?

The IBGI is a cantonal tax that applies when you sell a property for an amount exceeding its purchase price. **In short: you are taxed on the profit made between the time you acquired the property and the time you sell it.**

## How is it calculated?

The tax amount depends primarily on three elements:

- The gain realised: This is the difference between the sale price and the purchase price, from which certain expenses can be deducted (justified renovation work, acquisition costs, agency commissions, etc.).
- The tax scale: The rate applied to the real estate gain starts at 50% and decreases according to a degressive scale set by the canton.
- The holding period: The longer you hold the property, the lower the applicable rate. After 25 years of ownership, the rate is reduced to the legal minimum of 2% in Geneva.

## Can this tax be reduced?

Yes, several strategies allow you to legally reduce the IBGI:

- Include all justified costs in the calculation (renovation work, notary fees, brokerage, etc.).
- Opt for tax deferral (reinvestment) if you reinvest in a primary residence.
- Plan the sale to cross certain holding period thresholds.

At GARY, we support you well beyond the sale: we work with you to optimise the tax aspects in order to maximise your net gain.

Concrete example:

You purchase a property in 2014 for CHF 1,000,000. In 2024, you sell it for CHF 1,400,000. After deducting eligible expenses (renovation work, notary fees, brokerage, etc.), your net taxable gain amounts to CHF 300,000.

You have held the property for **10 years**. According to the 2025 Canton of Geneva schedule, this duration entitles you to a reduced rate of **10%** instead of the base rate of 50%.

**Estimated IBGI calculation:**

CHF 300,000 (gain) x 10% = **CHF 30,000 in tax payable**

## Why does this matter?

Too often, sellers discover the IBGI amount once the sale is concluded. This can disrupt your reinvestment plan, reduce your margins or create unnecessary financial strain. Proper preparation makes all the difference.

## Are you selling your property?

Do not let the IBGI erode your profits. Contact us for a free valuation of your property and the potential tax.

At GARY, our local expertise becomes your strategic advantage.

title: Selling your property in 2025: why an exclusive mandate makes all the difference
description: Even today, many property owners believe that by entrusting their property to several agencies, they will multiply their chances of a quick sale. This idea, although widespread, is far from reflecting market reality.
content:
**April 2025**

Even today, many property owners believe that by entrusting their property to several agencies, they will multiply their chances of a quick sale. This idea, although widespread, is **far from reflecting market reality**.

## The false promise of a non-exclusive mandate

"More agencies = more visibility = faster sale."

This is the classic reasoning. But in practice, the **opposite** often occurs.

A property entrusted to multiple agencies ends up **listed in duplicate** on platforms, sometimes with different photos, **inconsistent descriptions**, or even a price that varies. This creates confusion among buyers... and **directly harms the perceived value of the property**.

What was supposed to boost the sale often ends up slowing it down. Viewings become rare, buyers negotiate harder, and owners lose time... and sometimes money.

## Exclusivity means clarity

Conversely, an exclusive mandate allows you to build a genuine sales strategy. A single agency is in charge of the property, **which guarantees a unified message, a controlled presentation and targeted marketing.**

It is also a strong commitment from the agency: it invests time, resources and expertise to sell under the best conditions. At GARY, an exclusive mandate is systematically accompanied by professional photography, clear market positioning and rigorous support at every stage.

## Concrete results

This model delivers results. Properties entrusted under an exclusive mandate generally sell **more quickly**, with **less negotiation**, and under **optimal conditions for the seller**.

Why? Because the buyer also perceives this exclusivity as a mark of seriousness. They receive clear information, a consistent price and a smoother search experience. **This builds trust — and accelerates the decision**.

## Selling well means choosing well

Opting for an exclusive mandate does not mean giving up freedom: quite the contrary. It means choosing a structured, personalised approach that is above all results-oriented. It also means giving yourself the means to sell quickly, well and without stress.

At GARY, we do not sign a mandate just to "have" it, but to sell it. And we commit 200%, with transparency, method and precise knowledge of the local market.

**Contact our experts** and discover concretely how we can support you in your property project.

gary@gary.ch

title: CASATAX 2025: a tax opportunity not to be missed for future property owners
description: Becoming a property owner in Geneva is a significant challenge. Between high prices and property scarcity, every advantage counts. Among them, CASATAX stands out as a powerful tax lever to reduce acquisition costs. Discover how this measure can turn your property project into reality.
content:
**March 2025**

Becoming a property owner in Geneva is a significant challenge. Between high prices and property scarcity, every advantage counts. Among them, CASATAX stands out as a powerful tax lever to **reduce acquisition costs**. Discover how this measure can turn your property project into reality.

## What is CASATAX?

CASATAX is a Geneva cantonal initiative, in force since 2004, aimed at facilitating access to property ownership. It offers a **significant reduction in costs associated with purchasing a property intended as a primary residence**.

Specifically, it allows you to reduce:

- Registration duties upon purchase.
- Costs of creating the mortgage note, representing a significant portion of acquisition costs.

## What's new for 2025

From 1 March 2025, CASATAX evolves to adapt to the Geneva property market:

- Property price ceiling: CHF 1,374,396 (compared to CHF 1,359,903 in 2024).
- Reduction in registration duties: CHF 20,616 (compared to CHF 20,399 in 2024).
- Reduction in mortgage note creation costs: up to 50% reduction

These adjustments, of approximately 1.1%, are based on the Geneva construction price index, reflecting the constant evolution of construction costs and property prices in the canton.

## Conditions for benefiting from CASATAX

To take advantage of this measure, certain conditions must be met:

- Property use: The property must be intended to serve as a primary residence.
- Residence commitment: The buyer commits to establishing their primary residence in the acquired property for at least three years, within two years of purchase.
- Price ceiling: The purchase price must not exceed CHF 1,374,396.
- Type of buyer: CASATAX is intended solely for natural persons.

In the event of non-compliance with these commitments, the buyer will be required to return all benefits obtained through CASATAX.

## Why CASATAX is an opportunity to seize?

In a tight property market such as Geneva's, CASATAX represents valuable support for buyers:

- Substantial savings: An immediate reduction in acquisition costs, with no advance of funds.
- Increased accessibility: A raised ceiling, allowing a wider range of properties to be included.
- Support for ownership: A targeted measure for residents wishing to acquire their principal dwelling.

## How GARY can support you

At GARY, we understand the challenges of the Geneva market and the importance of every advantage for our clients.

- Our team is at your disposal to: Identify properties eligible for CASATAX.
- Optimise your financing by taking tax reductions into account.
- Support you through all administrative procedures related to your acquisition.

Do not wait any longer to make your property project a reality with GARY.

Contact us today to benefit from our expertise and make your dream of ownership a reality.

gary@gary.ch

title: New key rate cut: towards a residential market revival?
description: On 21 March 2025, the Swiss National Bank (SNB) announced a new cut to its key interest rate, bringing it from 0.50% to 0.25%. This is the fifth consecutive cut since 2023, confirming the establishment of a genuine cycle of monetary easing.
content:
**March 2025**

## A fifth cut confirming the trend

On 21 March 2025, the Swiss National Bank (SNB) announced a new cut to its key interest rate, bringing it from **0.50% to 0.25%**. This is the fifth consecutive cut since 2023, confirming the establishment of a genuine cycle of monetary easing.

While these decisions are no longer a surprise, their effects on the Swiss residential property market remain very tangible. In a country where the majority of purchases rely on mortgage financing, **each adjustment to the key rate directly impacts market dynamics.**

## Buyer confidence returns, projects restart

After a period of caution linked to rising rates between 2022 and mid-2023, many households are today regaining **room to manoeuvre**. Viewings are picking up, financing applications are multiplying, and projects that were on hold are back on the table.

"You can sense that people have readjusted their expectations, and that the continued rate cuts are giving them visibility and peace of mind," notes Guive Emami, MRICS, Partner at GARY.

The most visible impacts:

- Easier access to credit
- More competitive mortgage rates
- Recovering demand, particularly in urban centres

**Meet our experts and financing partners today.**

## Sellers also have a card to play

For owners wishing to sell, this dynamic represents a **strategic window** not to be overlooked. The market remains demanding, but more active demand often makes it possible to **reduce sales timelines** and optimise transaction conditions.

What we are observing on the ground:

- More viewings, more serious enquiries
- Importance of a well-presented and accurately valued property
- Good timing, before a mass return of sellers to the market

**Whether you are considering an upgrade or a downgrade, now is the right time to get ahead.**

## Three tips for navigating this phase wisely

- Act without waiting for the general rush: it is often during these transition periods that the best opportunities arise.
- Reassess your purchasing capacity: new rate conditions may open new perspectives. For a tenant, is the monthly mortgage repayment equivalent to or even lower than the rent?
- Refine your sales strategy: presentation, price, positioning... every detail can make the difference.

## Key takeaways

The market today offers the most favourable conditions seen in three years — an environment conducive to informed decisions and strategic moves.

The key rate cut to 0.25% reinforces an already more favourable climate for residential property. This is not a return to euphoria, but a clear signal: **the market is easing, and those who act now could benefit from a more balanced and responsive environment.**

At GARY, we follow these developments as closely as possible on the ground, with practical advice and a long-term vision. Because in a moving market, it is lucid and well-prepared decisions that make the difference.

**Contact our experts for more information.**

gary@gary.ch
